doc_description: This document provides a comprehensive overview of the DigestiveDataSets package, detailing its curated collection of diverse datasets related to the digestive system and gastrointestinal diseases, along with specific examples and visualizations to aid in research and clinical practice.

structure.title: DigestiveDataSets: A Curated Collection of Digestive System and Gastrointestinal Disease Datasets
structure.summary: The partial document describes the DigestiveDataSets package, which is a curated collection of datasets related to the digestive system and gastrointestinal diseases. It highlights the variety of data types included, such as clinical trials, observational studies, and cohort data, covering a wide range of conditions like gastritis, ulcers, pancreatitis, and colorectal diseases. The datasets are intended for use in education, clinical practice, and biomedical research, particularly benefiting fields like gastroenterology, public health, and epidemiology. Additionally, the document lists specific datasets available within the package, showcasing the diversity of topics covered.
structure.text:
DigestiveDataSets: A Curated
Collection of Digestive System and
Gastrointestinal Disease Datasets
Introduction
The DigestiveDataSets  package offers a comprehensive and curated collection of datasets
focused on the digestive system, including the stomach, intestines, liver, pancreas, and
related disorders . This package encompasses a variety of data types such as clinical trials,
observational studies, experimental datasets, cohort data, and case series, providing broad
coverage of gastrointestinal diseases.
Included datasets span a range of conditions such as gastritis, ulcers, pancreatitis, liver
cirrhosis, colorectal diseases, colon cancer, Helicobacter pylori infection, irritable
bowel syndrome, intestinal infections, and post-surgical outcomes . These datasets serve
multiple purposes in education, clinical practice, and biomedical research, and are particularly
valuable in fields such as gastroenterology, public health, and epidemiology.
All datasets within DigestiveDataSetslibrary(DigestiveDataSets)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
#> 
#> Adjuntando el paquete: 'dplyr'
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
#> 
#>     filter, lag
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
#> 
#>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
view_datasets_DigestiveDataSets ()
#>  [1] "anorexia_weight_change_df"     "bleeding_ulcers_df"           
#>  [3] "campylobacter_infections_ts"   "cholera_deaths_1849_tbl_df"   
#>  [5] "colon_stageBC_chemo_df"        "colonoscopy_features_tbl_df"  
#>  [7] "crc_mirnas_pubmed_tbl_df"      "cystic_fibrosis_snps_df"      
#>  [9] "digestive_cancer_survival_df"  "ecoli_infections_df"          
#> [11] "gastric_cancer_trial_df"       "gi_damage_prevention_df"      
#> [13] "helicobacter_children_tbl_df"  "horse_colic_surgery_df"       
#> [15] "ibs_cam_trials_df"             "intestinal_smartpill_df"

nodes.title: Dataset Suffixes
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the dataset suffixes used in the DigestiveDataSets package, which categorize R objects into types such as data frames, tibbles, and time series. It provides examples of datasets included in the package, such as "digestive_cancer_survival_df" for cancer survival times and "campylobacter_infections_ts" for infection time series. Additionally, it outlines a minimal data processing example using the "digestive_cancer_survival_df" dataset to summarize cancer cases and convert the data into a format suitable for plotting. Finally, it includes a ggplot visualization comparing stomach and colon cancer cases, highlighting the use of specific colors and a minimal theme for the plot.
nodes.text:
Dataset Suffixes
Each dataset in the DigestiveDataSets  package uses a suffix to denote the type of R object:
_df: data frame
_tbl_df: tibble
_ts: time series
Below are selected example datasets included in the DigestiveDataSets  package:
digestive_cancer_survival_df : Digestive Cancer Survival Times.
campylobacter_infections_ts : Campylobacter Infections Time Series.
cholera_deaths_1849_tbl_df : Cholera Daily Deaths in England, 1849.
Data Visualization with DigestiveDataSets Data
Digestive Cancer Survival Times#> [17] "intestinal_surgery_df"         "liver_cirrhosis_prednisone_df"
#> [19] "lynch_ontario_families_df"     "norovirus_derbyshire_df"      
#> [21] "pancreatic_cancer_df"          "pbc_mayo_survival_df"         
#> [23] "post_ercp_pancreatitis_tbl_df" "ugi_bleeding_df"              
#> [25] "weight_loss_df"
◦ 
◦ 
◦ 
◦ 
◦ 
◦ 
# Minimal data processing WITHOUT tidyr
digestive_cancer_survival_df %>%
summarise (
Stomach = sum(stomach, na.rm = TRUE),
Colon = sum(colon, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
# Convert to plot-ready format WITHOUT tidyr
  {data.frame (
Cancer = names(.),
Cases = unlist(., use.names = FALSE)
  )} %>%
# Plot
ggplot(aes(x = Cancer, y = Cases)) +
geom_col (fill =c("#e63946" , "#1d3557" )) +
labs(title = "Stomach vs Colon Cancer Cases" ) +
theme_minimal ()

nodes.title: Digestive Cancer Survival Times
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the dataset suffixes used in the DigestiveDataSets package, which categorize R objects into types such as data frames, tibbles, and time series. It provides examples of datasets included in the package, such as "digestive_cancer_survival_df" for cancer survival times, "campylobacter_infections_ts" for infection time series, and "cholera_deaths_1849_tbl_df" for historical death data. Additionally, it outlines a minimal data processing example using the "digestive_cancer_survival_df" dataset to summarize cancer cases and convert the data into a plot-ready format, followed by a ggplot visualization comparing stomach and colon cancer cases.
nodes.text:
Dataset Suffixes
Each dataset in the DigestiveDataSets  package uses a suffix to denote the type of R object:
_df: data frame
_tbl_df: tibble
_ts: time series
Below are selected example datasets included in the DigestiveDataSets  package:
digestive_cancer_survival_df : Digestive Cancer Survival Times.
campylobacter_infections_ts : Campylobacter Infections Time Series.
cholera_deaths_1849_tbl_df : Cholera Daily Deaths in England, 1849.
Data Visualization with DigestiveDataSets Data
Digestive Cancer Survival Times#> [17] "intestinal_surgery_df"         "liver_cirrhosis_prednisone_df"
#> [19] "lynch_ontario_families_df"     "norovirus_derbyshire_df"      
#> [21] "pancreatic_cancer_df"          "pbc_mayo_survival_df"         
#> [23] "post_ercp_pancreatitis_tbl_df" "ugi_bleeding_df"              
#> [25] "weight_loss_df"
◦ 
◦ 
◦ 
◦ 
◦ 
◦ 
# Minimal data processing WITHOUT tidyr
digestive_cancer_survival_df %>%
summarise (
Stomach = sum(stomach, na.rm = TRUE),
Colon = sum(colon, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
# Convert to plot-ready format WITHOUT tidyr
  {data.frame (
Cancer = names(.),
Cases = unlist(., use.names = FALSE)
  )} %>%
# Plot
ggplot(aes(x = Cancer, y = Cases)) +
geom_col (fill =c("#e63946" , "#1d3557" )) +
labs(title = "Stomach vs Colon Cancer Cases" ) +
theme_minimal ()

nodes.title: Campylobacter Infections Time Series.
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the analysis of Campylobacter infections over time using a time series dataset. It describes the conversion of the time series data into a numeric format for both infections and time. A plot is created using ggplot to visualize the number of Campylobacter infections over time, featuring a line and points in steel blue color. The plot includes labels for the title and axes, and it employs a minimal theme for clarity.
nodes.text:
Campylobacter Infections Time Series.
# Convert ts to numeric vector and numeric time
campy_df <-data.frame (
infections = as.numeric (campylobacter_infections_ts),
time =as.numeric (time(campylobacter_infections_ts))
)
# Plot without warning by using numeric 'time'
ggplot(campy_df, aes(x = time, y = infections)) +
geom_line (color = "steelblue" ) +
geom_point (color = "steelblue" ) +
labs(
title = "Campylobacter Infections Over Time" ,
x ="Time (year.fraction)" ,
y ="Number of Infections"
  ) +
theme_minimal ()

nodes.title: Cholera Daily Deaths in England, 1849.
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a data visualization of cholera deaths in England during the year 1849. It describes the use of the ggplot2 package in R to create a line graph that illustrates the number of deaths over time, with the x-axis representing dates and the y-axis indicating the number of deaths. The graph also differentiates deaths by the cause of death using color coding. The overall theme of the visualization is minimalistic, focusing on clarity and ease of interpretation.
nodes.text:
Cholera Daily Deaths in England, 1849.
ggplot(cholera_deaths_1849_tbl_df, aes(x = date, y = deaths, color =
cause_of_death)) +
geom_line () +
labs(
title = "Cholera Deaths Over Time in 1849" ,
x ="Date",
y ="Number of Deaths" ,
color = "Cause of Death"
  ) +
theme_minimal ()

structure.title: Conclusion
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the DigestiveDataSets package, highlighting its extensive collection of datasets related to the digestive system and associated diseases. It emphasizes the inclusion of various types of data, such as clinical trials and observational studies, which cater to diverse applications in research, education, and clinical practice. Additionally, it mentions the availability of detailed information and documentation for each dataset within the package.
structure.text:
Conclusion
The DigestiveDataSets  package offers a comprehensive, well-curated collection of datasets
focused on the digestive system and related diseases. By including clinical trials, observational
studies, cohort data, and experimental datasets covering a broad spectrum of gastrointestinal
conditions, this package supports a wide range of applications in research, education, and
clinical practice.
For detailed information and full documentation of each dataset, please refer to the reference
manual and help files included within the package.
